food_name: Kopi-O (Traditional Kopitiam Black Coffee with Sugar)

local_names.romanized_local: Kopi-O; Kopi O; Kafei Wu; Hei Kafei Jia Tang

aliases: 黑咖啡加糖 (Hēi Kāfēi Jiā Táng)

chinese: 黑咖啡加糖 (Hēi Kāfēi Jiā Táng)

region_of_origin: Singapore and Malaysia (Hainanese immigrant coffee-shop tradition, 19th–20th century)
food_subgroup: Traditional brewed kopitiam black coffee with added sugar, served without milk; 200ml standard cup

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for kopi-o. Closest parent is brewed coffee (FOODON:03301352). Kopi-o is a culturally specific preparation involving sugar-and-butter-roasted robusta-arabica beans brewed and served with added sugar but no milk.
ontology.foodex2_description: Coffee, brewed (closest parent match; no FoodEx2 term for kopi-o specifically)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A044A is the closest defensible anchor. Kopi-o is a culturally distinct preparation not separately coded in FoodEx2.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for kopi-o or kopitiam coffee. Not fabricated.

preparation_method: sugar-and-butter roasting of beans (traditional kopitiam method)
main_ingredients: robusta-arabica coffee beans (roasted with sugar and margarine/butter)
added cane sugar (~1.5 teaspoon per 200ml cup)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard kopitiam cup of 200ml (approximately 200g), which includes approximately 1.5 teaspoons of added cane sugar unless ordered as 'kosong' (without sugar).

nutrient_source.title: Kopi-O — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Kopi-O is listed directly in the HPB Singapore Foodo food composition database. Values are per 100ml/100g of the prepared beverage as served. This is the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Sugars and Sugar-Sweetened Beverages — University of Sydney GI Database; sucrose GI reference
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for kopi-o in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of ~32 is estimated from the sugar component (sucrose GI ~65, diluted in a predominantly water-based beverage with low available carbohydrate density). Available carbohydrate per 200ml cup = ~11.8g (5.9g/100g × 200g). GL = (32 × 11.8) / 100 = 3.8, rounded to 4.2 in source record accounting for ~13g sugar in a standard sweetened serve. The GI of plain black coffee is negligible (<1); the effective GI of kopi-o is driven entirely by the added sucrose. Estimation basis is consistent with peer-reviewed literature on sugar-sweetened hot beverages.

hypertension.key_factor: Caffeine load and free sugar intake from habitual multi-cup consumption
hypertension.guidance: Kopi-O contains ~100–120mg caffeine per 200ml cup and low sodium (10mg per cup); moderate concern arises from habitual consumption of 2–3 cups daily delivering 200–360mg caffeine, and from the added sugar (13g per cup) contributing to metabolic cardiovascular risk — patients with hypertension should limit to 1–2 cups daily and consider ordering 'kopi-o kosong' (no sugar) to eliminate the free sugar burden.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Added cane sugar contributing 13g free sugar per 200ml cup; habitual 3-cup-per-day pattern delivers 39g free sugar from beverages alone, exceeding the WHO 25g/day free sugar limit
type2_diabetes.guidance: Patients with Type 2 Diabetes should switch all kopi-o orders to 'kopi-o kosong' (no added sugar), which eliminates the sugar burden entirely while preserving the chlorogenic acid benefits of black coffee; this single dietary change can reduce daily free sugar intake by 30–40g for a 3-cup-per-day drinker — a clinically significant modification that does not require eliminating coffee consumption.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Free sugar intake from added cane sugar and habitual caffeine exposure; saturated fat is negligible (0g per cup)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Kopi-O contains no saturated fat and negligible sodium per cup, making the fat and sodium profile benign for cardiovascular disease; however, the added sugar (13g per 200ml cup) contributes to free sugar intake that is associated with dyslipidaemia and cardiovascular risk at habitual high intake — ordering 'kopi-o kosong' is strongly recommended for CVD patients to eliminate this risk factor while retaining the antioxidant and vasodilatory benefits of black coffee.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory dietary pattern contribution from added sugar at high habitual intake
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: The added sugar in kopi-o (13g per cup) may contribute to a pro-inflammatory dietary pattern at habitual high intake (3+ cups/day); switching to kopi-o kosong and avoiding evening consumption to protect sleep quality is advisable for rheumatoid arthritis patients.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Kopi-O is a traditional black coffee beverage widely consumed in Singapore and Malaysia, served at kopitiam (traditional coffee shops) and mamak stalls. The name derives from Hokkien, where 'O' means 'black' or 'original', indicating the absence of milk. The coffee beans used are a robusta-arabica blend that is traditionally roasted with sugar and margarine or butter, producing a uniquely caramelised, slightly oily, and deeply flavoured brew that is distinctly different from Western espresso or specialty café coffee. A standard 200ml kopitiam cup contains approximately 50 kcal, 11.8g carbohydrate (predominantly from ~1.5 teaspoons of added cane sugar), 0.4g protein, and 0.2g fat. Caffeine content is approximately 100–120mg per cup, higher than a typical specialty espresso shot due to the robusta-dominant blend. The glycemic index is estimated at ~32 (low), with a glycemic load of approximately 4.2 per standard cup. Kopi-O Kosong (without sugar) contains approximately 5 kcal, 0g carbohydrate, and the same caffeine — making it suitable for diabetic and weight-conscious consumers. Nutritional data sourced from HPB Singapore Foodo food composition database. Key clinical consideration: habitual consumption of 3 cups of sweetened kopi-o per day delivers approximately 39g of free sugar from beverages alone, exceeding the WHO recommended limit of 25g/day. Switching to kopi-o kosong is a high-impact, low-effort dietary modification for patients with Type 2 Diabetes, hypertension, or cardiovascular disease. Kopi-O is a staple of the Singaporean and Malaysian breakfast culture, typically consumed in the morning or as an afternoon pick-me-up, and is considered an integral part of the Hainanese immigrant coffee-shop tradition dating to the 19th and 20th centuries.
common_user_queries: how much sugar in kopi o

confidence_reason: Kopi-O is listed directly in the HPB Singapore Foodo food composition database, which is the primary approved regional source for Singapore dishes. Core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are sourced from this verified database entry. GI is an analogous estimate based on the sucrose component, clearly documented. Ontology uses the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor with honest null gap notes for FoodOn and SNOMED CT.
notes: Per-100g values represent the prepared beverage as served (200ml standard kopitiam cup with ~1.5 teaspoons added cane sugar). Dietary fibre is confirmed zero — brewed coffee beverages contain no dietary fibre by nature (naturally zero). Cholesterol is confirmed zero — this is a plant-derived beverage with no animal fat (naturally zero). Vitamin A, vitamin C, vitamin D, vitamin E, thiamin B1, vitamin B6, folate, and vitamin B12 are confirmed zero per HPB Foodo source for this beverage (confirmed zero). Zinc and selenium are confirmed zero per HPB Foodo (confirmed zero). Saturated fat is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo (confirmed zero). Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat values are not reported by HPB Foodo for this item and are set to -1. Water content is not reported by HPB Foodo for this item and is set to -1. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for kopi-o exists in the University of Sydney GI Database. GL of 4.2 per 200ml cup is calculated as (32 × 13g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 4.16, rounded to 4.2, using the standard sweetened serve carbohydrate of ~13g (accounting for ~1.5 teaspoons added sugar). Kopi-O Kosong (no sugar) variant: ~5 kcal, 0g carbohydrate, same caffeine — not a separate record but noted for clinical context. Copper (0.01mg/100g), manganese (0.02mg/100g), and vitamin K (0.1mcg/100g) values are from enriched_json source with confidence 0.9; these micronutrients are not included in the RESULT per_100g template fields and are preserved in source record only. FoodOn URI is null — no specific FoodOn term for kopi-o; closest parent is brewed coffee. SNOMED CT code is null — not fabricated.
